steps.text: You learned to choose scales. You checked if the biggest number fits. You checked the last digits. Let's see what you know.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 1: doesn't recognize that the scale won't fit the data

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Ben collected 70 toys. Scale of 1 would need 70 lines on your graph. That's way too many to fit.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 2: doesn't recognize that the scale won't fit the data

steps.text: You're working on it. Scale of 2 would need 35 lines to reach 70. That's still too many lines to fit on one graph.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 5: doesn't recognize that the scale won't fit the data

steps.text: You're getting there. The key is that Scale of 5 would need 14 lines to show 70. That's better than 1 or 2, but still won't fit.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Scale of 10 highlights and pulses to show it's the correct choice

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. Ben collected 70 toys, the highest number. Scale of 10 only needs 8 lines to reach 70. Scales of 1, 2, and 5 all need too many lines. Scale of 10 fits best.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 5: assumes all numbers fit in range, missing that exact values require checking ones place digits

steps.text: Let's think about this together. Check the ones place digits: 3, 1, 9, 6. Are these multiples of 5? Only multiples of 5 end in 0 or 5.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 10: assumes larger scale always works, missing that exact values require checking ones place digits

steps.text: Here's a hint: look at the ones place of each value. 23, 31, 9, 16. To show these exactly, your scale must count by a number that gets you to 3, 1, 9, and 6.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Ones place digits (3, 1, 9, 6) pulse to show they are not multiples of 5 or 10

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. The values are 23, 31, 9, 16. Look at the ones place: 3, 1, 9, 6. None end in 0 or 5, so they aren't multiples of 5 or 10. Scale of 2 counts by 2 and can show all these values exactly.

condition.selected: Scale of 5 — it shows more detail.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 5: misconception that more detail is always better

steps.text: You're working on it. All the numbers end in 0, so Scale of 5 would create extra lines you don't need. Which scale matches these numbers best?

condition.selected: Scale of 2 — it works for any numbers.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 2: misconception that smallest scale is always appropriate

steps.text: Let's think about this together. To reach 90 with Scale of 2, you'd need 45 lines. That's too many. What scale fits these numbers better?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: All values pulse briefly to show they end in 0

steps.text: Here, let me walk you through this one. Look at the numbers: 30, 70, 90, 50. They all end in 0. Scale of 10 matches perfectly. It gives you just 10 lines to reach 90.
